nodes.label: Genomic Instability
nodes.description: Accumulation of DNA damage throughout life is a major driver of aging. Nuclear DNA is continuously exposed to endogenous (reactive oxygen species, replication errors, spontaneous hydrolysis) and exogenous (UV, ionizing radiation, chemical mutagens) genotoxic stresses. Although organisms have evolved sophisticated DNA damage response (DDR) and repair mechanisms, these systems become less efficient with age, leading to progressive genomic instability manifested as point mutations, chromosomal rearrangements, telomere shortening, and epigenetic changes. Persistent DNA damage activates senescence programs and ultimately drives tissue dysfunction.

nodes.label: Telomere Attrition
nodes.description: Telomeres are nucleoprotein structures protecting chromosome ends from degradation and illegitimate recombination. With each cell division, telomeres shorten due to the end-replication problem and oxidative damage. When telomeres reach a critically short length, they trigger a persistent DDR leading to senescence or apoptosis. Telomerase, the enzyme that elongates telomeres, is expressed primarily in stem cells and germ cells but is largely absent in somatic tissues. Short telomeres are strongly associated with organismal aging, age-related diseases, and decreased lifespan in humans and model organisms.

nodes.label: Epigenetic Alterations
nodes.description: Age-related changes in the epigenome include alterations in DNA methylation patterns, histone modifications, chromatin remodeling, and non-coding RNA expression. Global DNA hypomethylation occurs alongside locus-specific hypermethylation at CpG islands. Histone marks such as H3K4me3, H3K27me3, and H4K16ac change quantitatively with age. These epigenetic drifts alter gene expression programs, activate transposable elements, and disrupt heterochromatin structure. Epigenetic clocks based on DNA methylation patterns (Horvath clock, Hannum clock, PhenoAge, GrimAge) can predict biological age more accurately than chronological age.

nodes.label: Loss of Proteostasis
nodes.description: Proteostasis refers to the integrated network of pathways that control protein synthesis, folding, trafficking, and degradation. This network includes molecular chaperones (HSP70, HSP90, HSP40 families), the ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS), and autophagy-lysosomal pathway. With aging, proteostasis becomes progressively compromised: chaperone capacity declines, proteasome activity decreases, and autophagic flux is impaired. This leads to the accumulation of misfolded, oxidized, and aggregated proteins. Proteotoxic stress is central to numerous age-related diseases including neurodegeneration and cataracts.

nodes.label: Disabled Macroautophagy
nodes.description: Macroautophagy is a lysosome-dependent bulk degradation pathway that selectively targets damaged organelles (mitophagy, ER-phagy), protein aggregates (aggrephagy), and intracellular pathogens (xenophagy) for recycling. Autophagy is essential for cellular homeostasis and its impairment leads to accumulation of damaged cellular components, promoting aging and disease. With age, autophagosome formation, lysosomal acidification, and autophagic flux all decline. Genetic enhancement of autophagy extends lifespan in multiple organisms. Disabled macroautophagy was elevated to hallmark status in 2023 due to compelling evidence of its independent contribution to aging.

nodes.label: Deregulated Nutrient Sensing
nodes.description: Four main nutrient-sensing systems govern metabolic homeostasis and their deregulation promotes aging: insulin/IGF-1 signaling (IIS), target of rapamycin (TOR), AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), and sirtuins. Reduced IIS extends lifespan in diverse organisms. TOR integrates nutrient signals and promotes anabolism while suppressing autophagy; mTORC1 hyperactivation promotes aging while rapamycin extends lifespan. AMPK acts as an energy sensor activated by caloric restriction and metformin, promoting catabolic processes. Sirtuins (SIRT1-7) are NAD+-dependent deacylases that integrate metabolic status with epigenetic regulation and stress responses. Age-related decline in AMPK activity and sirtuin expression impairs metabolic adaptation.

nodes.label: Mitochondrial Dysfunction
nodes.description: Mitochondria are central to cellular energy production, calcium homeostasis, reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, and apoptosis regulation. With aging, mitochondrial function declines through multiple mechanisms: accumulation of mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) mutations and deletions, reduced biogenesis (PGC-1α pathway), impaired mitophagy, decreased electron transport chain (ETC) complex activities, and altered mitochondrial dynamics (fusion/fission balance). The resulting increase in ROS production leads to oxidative damage to proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids. Dysfunctional mitochondria also trigger sterile inflammation through release of mtDNA and other damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs).

nodes.label: Cellular Senescence
nodes.description: Cellular senescence is a stable cell cycle arrest state triggered by diverse stresses including telomere shortening (replicative senescence), oncogene activation (oncogene-induced senescence), oxidative damage, and genotoxic stress. Senescent cells resist apoptosis and develop the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP), secreting pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8), chemokines, matrix metalloproteinases, and growth factors. While senescence initially serves as a tumor suppressor and wound healing mechanism, the chronic accumulation of senescent cells with age creates a pro-inflammatory tissue environment that drives age-related dysfunction, tissue degeneration, and cancer progression in surrounding cells.

nodes.label: Stem Cell Exhaustion
nodes.description: Tissue homeostasis depends on the regenerative capacity of somatic stem cells (SSCs), which replenish differentiated cells throughout life. With aging, SSCs decline in number and/or functional capacity across multiple compartments: hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) show myeloid skewing and clonal hematopoiesis; intestinal stem cells (ISCs) lose regenerative capacity; muscle satellite cells decline in number and self-renewal; neural stem cells decrease in proliferative capacity. Stem cell exhaustion is driven by accumulated DNA damage, epigenetic alterations, telomere shortening, mitochondrial dysfunction, and altered niche signaling. This results in impaired tissue repair, reduced organ function, and increased susceptibility to diseases.
biomarkers: HSC frequency and function (colony forming unit)

nodes.label: Altered Intercellular Communication
nodes.description: Aging is associated with broad changes in cell-to-cell communication at local and systemic levels. This includes alterations in endocrine, paracrine, and neuronal signaling. The aging plasma environment contains increased pro-inflammatory factors (inflammaging) and decreased regenerative factors. Parabiosis experiments demonstrated that young blood can rejuvenate old tissues. Key aging-related changes include increased NF-κB-driven pro-inflammatory signaling, dysregulated hormonal axes (GH/IGF-1, sex hormones, thyroid), altered extracellular vesicle content and secretion, and modified synaptic transmission in the nervous system. Loss of appropriate communication leads to tissue dysfunction, impaired regeneration, and systemic metabolic dysregulation.

nodes.label: Chronic Inflammation (Inflammaging)
nodes.description: Inflammaging describes the chronic, sterile, low-grade inflammatory state that develops with advancing age and drives most age-related diseases. Unlike acute inflammation, inflammaging is characterized by persistent elevation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-18, TNF-α) without an overt infection or injury trigger. Sources include accumulation of senescent cells (SASP), activation of the cGAS-STING pathway by cytosolic DNA (from nuclear and mitochondrial damage), gut dysbiosis-induced bacterial translocation, chronic activation of pattern recognition receptors (TLRs, NLRPs), and macrophage polarization toward M1 phenotype. Inflammaging is now recognized as a distinct hallmark due to its pervasive, causative role in aging pathology.

nodes.label: Dysbiosis
nodes.description: The gut microbiome undergoes substantial compositional and functional changes with aging, a process termed dysbiosis. Age-related dysbiosis is characterized by decreased microbial diversity, loss of beneficial taxa (Bifidobacterium, Lactobacillus, Faecalibacterium prausnitzii), expansion of potentially pathogenic bacteria, reduced short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) production, and increased intestinal permeability. These changes promote systemic inflammation through bacterial translocation, alter immune system education and function, impair nutrient absorption, and dysregulate gut-brain axis signaling. Centenarians show distinct microbiome signatures associated with longevity, supporting the causal role of dysbiosis in aging. Fecal microbiota transplantation from young to old organisms improves aging phenotypes in experimental models.

nodes.label: Oxidative Stress
nodes.description: Imbalance between reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and antioxidant defenses. ROS generated primarily by mitochondrial electron transport chain leak oxidize lipids (lipid peroxidation), proteins (carbonylation), and DNA (8-OHdG formation). Chronic oxidative stress accelerates genomic instability, protein misfolding, mitochondrial dysfunction, and activates inflammatory signaling through NF-κB. Age-related decline in Nrf2 signaling reduces antioxidant capacity, further exacerbating oxidative burden.

nodes.label: Neuroinflammation
nodes.description: Chronic activation of microglia (brain-resident macrophages) and astrocytes resulting in sustained release of pro-inflammatory mediators (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, NO, prostaglandins) in the central nervous system. Aging microglia adopt a dystrophic or sensitized phenotype with exaggerated inflammatory responses. Neuroinflammation impairs synaptic plasticity, promotes neuronal death, and synergizes with protein aggregation pathologies. The blood-brain barrier (BBB) becomes increasingly permeable with age, allowing peripheral inflammatory signals to amplify CNS inflammation.

nodes.label: Insulin Resistance / Metabolic Syndrome
nodes.description: Reduced cellular responsiveness to insulin signaling, impairing glucose uptake in skeletal muscle, liver, and adipose tissue. Insulin resistance develops through multiple mechanisms: lipotoxicity from ectopic fat deposition, inflammatory cytokine interference with insulin receptor substrate phosphorylation, mitochondrial dysfunction impairing fatty acid oxidation, and impaired GLUT4 translocation. Systemic insulin resistance leads to hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia, hypertension, and compensatory hyperinsulinemia, collectively termed metabolic syndrome.

nodes.label: mTOR Hyperactivation
nodes.description: Chronic hyperactivation of mechanistic target of rapamycin complex 1 (mTORC1) drives accelerated aging by suppressing autophagy, promoting anabolic processes that cause cellular hypertrophy and senescence, and impairing stress response pathways. mTORC1 hyperactivation blunts the adaptive response to nutrient scarcity, interferes with protein quality control through autophagy suppression, and promotes mitochondrial dysfunction. Rapamycin-mediated mTOR inhibition extends lifespan in multiple organisms and shows promise in age-related disease contexts.

nodes.label: NF-κB Signaling Dysregulation
nodes.description: Nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-κB) is a master transcription factor controlling inflammatory gene expression. With aging, NF-κB becomes chronically activated due to accumulation of DAMPs (damaged cellular components), ROS, SASP factors, gut-derived endotoxins, and cGAS-STING pathway activation by cytosolic DNA. This drives persistent expression of pro-inflammatory genes (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, COX-2, iNOS) and promotes cellular senescence, creating a feed-forward inflammatory cycle that accelerates tissue deterioration.

nodes.label: Amyloid Aggregation
nodes.description: Aberrant aggregation of misfolded proteins into amyloid fibrils and oligomers. Amyloid-beta (Aβ) peptides derived from amyloid precursor protein (APP) by secretase cleavage misfold into oligomers and fibrils that deposit as senile plaques in Alzheimer's disease. Soluble Aβ oligomers are highly neurotoxic, impairing synaptic transmission, activating neuroinflammation, and inducing tau pathology. Islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) aggregation occurs in type 2 diabetes. General proteostasis collapse with age provides a permissive environment for amyloid formation across multiple disease contexts.

nodes.label: Tau Hyperphosphorylation
nodes.description: Tau is a microtubule-associated protein that stabilizes axonal microtubule networks. Hyperphosphorylation by kinases (GSK-3β, CDK5, DYRK1A) causes tau to detach from microtubules, misfold, and aggregate into neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs). NFTs disrupt axonal transport, impair synaptic function, and spread trans-synaptically through the brain in a prion-like manner following Braak staging. Tau pathology occurs in Alzheimer's disease, frontotemporal dementias, progressive supranuclear palsy, CTE, and other tauopathies. GSK-3β hyperactivation links insulin resistance to tau pathology.

nodes.label: Mitophagy Impairment
nodes.description: Mitophagy is the selective autophagic elimination of damaged mitochondria, primarily regulated by the PINK1-Parkin pathway and BNIP3/FUNDC1 receptors. Damaged mitochondria that fail to be cleared accumulate, generating excess ROS, releasing cytochrome c (triggering apoptosis), and releasing mtDNA that activates the cGAS-STING innate immune pathway. Age-related decline in PINK1 expression and Parkin activity, combined with lysosomal dysfunction, impairs mitophagy efficiency. Mitophagy impairment is particularly important in post-mitotic neurons, muscle cells, and cardiomyocytes that cannot dilute dysfunctional mitochondria by cell division.

nodes.label: Telomere-Induced DNA Damage Response
nodes.description: Critically short or dysfunctional telomeres are recognized as double-strand DNA breaks by the DNA damage response machinery, triggering persistent activation of ATM/ATR kinases, formation of gamma-H2AX foci, and recruitment of 53BP1 and other DDR factors. This telomeric DDR cannot be resolved by conventional repair mechanisms and induces irreversible cell cycle arrest (senescence) or apoptosis. Telomere-induced DDR is a major driver of replicative senescence in proliferating tissues and contributes to stem cell exhaustion. In post-mitotic cells, oxidative telomere damage can also activate a transient DDR that impairs cell function without inducing full senescence.

nodes.label: Senescence-Associated Secretory Phenotype (SASP)
nodes.description: The SASP is a complex secretome produced by senescent cells including pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1α, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8), chemokines (CXCL1, CXCL2, CCL2), matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-1, MMP-3, MMP-9), growth factors (amphiregulin, HGF), and bioactive lipids. SASP is regulated by NF-κB, C/EBPβ, and mTORC1 and is amplified by cytoplasmic chromatin fragments activating cGAS-STING. SASP drives paracrine senescence in neighboring cells, promotes cancer progression through immunosuppression and EMT induction, disrupts tissue architecture via MMP-mediated ECM remodeling, and drives systemic aging through endocrine-like effects. Senolytics (clearing senescent cells) and senomorphics (inhibiting SASP) are active therapeutic strategies.

nodes.label: Gut Permeability / Leaky Gut
nodes.description: Age-related degradation of intestinal barrier integrity, characterized by reduced tight junction protein expression (occludin, claudin-1, ZO-1), increased intestinal permeability, and translocation of bacteria, bacterial products (LPS, peptidoglycan), and metabolites into the systemic circulation. This chronic low-level endotoxemia activates TLR4 signaling in macrophages and other immune cells, driving systemic inflammation and contributing to inflammaging. Gut permeability is worsened by dysbiosis, reduced SCFA-producing bacteria (butyrate promotes epithelial integrity), decreased mucus production, and oxidative stress in enterocytes.

nodes.label: Short-Chain Fatty Acid Reduction
nodes.description: Short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs: butyrate, propionate, acetate) are produced by gut microbial fermentation of dietary fiber and serve as colonocyte energy sources, HDAC inhibitors (epigenetic regulation), and ligands for GPR43/GPR41/GPR109A receptors on immune cells. With aging and dysbiosis, SCFA-producing bacteria (Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Roseburia, Eubacterium rectale) decline, reducing SCFA production. Reduced butyrate impairs colonocyte energy metabolism, increases gut permeability, diminishes regulatory T cell induction, and promotes pro-inflammatory immune polarization. Butyrate supplementation shows rejuvenating effects in aging animal models.

nodes.label: Proteome Imbalance / Protein Aggregation
nodes.description: Age-related proteome imbalance results from the convergence of increased protein misfolding rates, reduced chaperone-mediated refolding capacity, impaired ubiquitin-proteasome system activity, and decreased autophagic clearance. Aggregation-prone proteins accumulate in cytoplasmic inclusions: alpha-synuclein in Lewy bodies (Parkinson's), huntingtin in striatal inclusions (Huntington's), TDP-43 and FUS in ALS, and SOD1 in familial ALS. These aggregates sequester functional proteins, disrupt vesicular trafficking, impair axonal transport, and trigger the unfolded protein response (UPR) of the ER.

nodes.label: Epigenetic Clock Acceleration
nodes.description: Biological age as measured by epigenetic clocks (Horvath, Hannum, PhenoAge, GrimAge) can accelerate beyond chronological age under conditions of chronic stress, obesity, smoking, sedentary lifestyle, and disease. Accelerated epigenetic aging reflects broader dysregulation of gene expression programs, loss of developmental epigenetic boundaries, and increased transcriptional noise. Clock acceleration correlates with all-cause mortality, cardiovascular disease, cancer, and neurodegeneration risk. Partial reprogramming with Yamanaka factors can reverse epigenetic age marks, suggesting epigenetic state is causally linked to organismal aging.

nodes.label: Clonal Hematopoiesis of Indeterminate Potential (CHIP)
nodes.description: Age-related somatic mutations in hematopoietic stem cells (particularly in DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1, JAK2) confer clonal fitness advantages, leading to expansion of mutant clones (clonal hematopoiesis). CHIP, present in >10% of adults over 70, is associated with increased risk of hematologic malignancies, but more broadly with cardiovascular disease, stroke, and all-cause mortality. TET2-mutant macrophages show exaggerated IL-1β and IL-6 production, directly linking CHIP to inflammaging. CHIP represents a convergence of genomic instability, epigenetic alterations, and altered intercellular communication.

nodes.label: Extracellular Matrix Stiffening / Fibrosis
nodes.description: Age-related changes in extracellular matrix (ECM) composition and crosslinking alter tissue biomechanical properties. Increased collagen crosslinking by lysyl oxidase and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) stiffens arteries, myocardium, and interstitial ECM. Progressive fibrosis driven by TGF-β signaling replaces functional parenchyma with collagen-rich scar tissue in liver (cirrhosis), lung (IPF), kidney (CKD), and heart (cardiac fibrosis). ECM stiffness signals through mechanosensory pathways (YAP/TAZ) to alter cell behavior, promote fibroblast-to-myofibroblast transition, and impair stem cell function.

nodes.label: Immunosenescence
nodes.description: Age-related decline in immune function affecting both innate and adaptive immunity. Thymic involution reduces naive T cell output, leading to contraction of the T cell receptor repertoire and accumulation of terminally differentiated, exhausted T cells (particularly CMV-specific CD8+ T cells, 'immunosenescent' T cells). B cell function declines, impairing vaccine responses and increasing autoantibody production. Innate immune cells show altered TLR signaling and defective pathogen killing despite enhanced baseline inflammation. Immunosenescence impairs cancer surveillance, increases susceptibility to infection, and reduces vaccine efficacy in older adults.

nodes.label: NAD+ Depletion
nodes.description: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD+) is an essential coenzyme for cellular energy metabolism (redox reactions in glycolysis, TCA cycle, oxidative phosphorylation) and a critical substrate for sirtuins (SIRT1-7) and PARP enzymes. NAD+ levels decline ~50% between youth and middle age due to increased consumption by PARP1 (activated by DNA damage), CD38 (an NADase upregulated by inflammation), and reduced biosynthesis from the salvage pathway. NAD+ depletion impairs sirtuin activity (reducing deacetylase functions in mitochondrial biogenesis, DNA repair, stress responses), reduces OXPHOS efficiency, and promotes inflammatory gene expression. NMN and NR supplementation can restore NAD+ levels.

nodes.label: Alpha-Synuclein Aggregation
nodes.description: Alpha-synuclein (αSyn) is a presynaptic protein that regulates dopamine release. Misfolding and aggregation of αSyn into oligomers and Lewy bodies is central to Parkinson's disease, Lewy body dementia, and multiple system atrophy (synucleinopathies). αSyn aggregation is promoted by oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired proteasomal/autophagic clearance, and post-translational modifications. Pathological αSyn spreads between neurons in a prion-like manner, explaining the progressive Braak staging of Parkinson's pathology. The gut-brain axis has emerged as an important route of αSyn propagation, originating in enteric neurons.

nodes.label: Vascular Inflammation and Endothelial Dysfunction
nodes.description: Age-related changes in the vasculature include endothelial dysfunction (reduced NO bioavailability), arterial stiffening (elastin degradation, collagen crosslinking), increased adhesion molecule expression (ICAM-1, VCAM-1), and intimal thickening. Endothelial cells become senescent and adopt a pro-inflammatory, pro-thrombotic phenotype. Vascular inflammation facilitates monocyte/macrophage infiltration and foam cell formation (atherosclerosis), impairs microvascular perfusion, increases cerebrovascular permeability (blood-brain barrier breakdown), and promotes cardiac hypertrophy through altered load conditions.

nodes.label: Lysosomal Dysfunction
nodes.description: Lysosomes are the primary degradative compartments receiving cargo from autophagy, endocytosis, and phagocytosis. Age-related lysosomal dysfunction is characterized by reduced cathepsin activity, increased lysosomal membrane permeabilization (LMP), elevated lysosomal pH (reduced acidification by V-ATPase), and accumulation of undegradable lipofuscin material. Dysfunctional lysosomes cannot complete autophagy, impairing clearance of damaged organelles and protein aggregates. TFEB, the master transcriptional regulator of lysosomal biogenesis, shows reduced nuclear translocation with age. Lysosomal storage disorders provide Mendelian models of how lysosomal failure drives aging-like pathology.

nodes.label: ER Stress / Unfolded Protein Response
nodes.description: Accumulation of misfolded proteins in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) triggers the unfolded protein response (UPR) through three sensor branches: IRE1α, PERK, and ATF6. The UPR aims to restore ER proteostasis through translational attenuation, chaperone upregulation, and ERAD (ER-associated degradation). With aging, chronic ER stress leads to persistent, maladaptive UPR activation that promotes apoptosis, inflammation (CHOP, NF-κB), and insulin resistance. ER stress is particularly important in secretory cells (β-cells, hepatocytes, plasma cells) that have high protein folding demands.

nodes.label: Lipid Peroxidation and Ferroptosis
nodes.description: Oxidative damage to polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) in cellular membranes generates reactive lipid aldehydes (4-HNE, malondialdehyde) and lipid peroxides that alter membrane fluidity, impair receptor function, and damage proteins and DNA. Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent regulated cell death program driven by GPX4 inactivation and uncontrolled lipid peroxidation, has emerged as a relevant cell death pathway in neurodegeneration, ischemia-reperfusion injury, and renal tubular cell death. Age-related accumulation of iron and decreased GPX4 activity increases ferroptotic vulnerability. Lipid peroxidation products serve as disease biomarkers.

nodes.label: Stem Cell Niche Dysfunction
nodes.description: Stem cell function depends critically on signals from their surrounding niche microenvironment. With aging, niche cells undergo senescence, altered secretory profiles, and ECM remodeling that compromise niche support function. In muscle, niche fibro-adipogenic progenitors (FAPs) shift from pro-regenerative to pro-fibrotic with age, impairing satellite cell activation. Bone marrow niches show increased adipogenesis and altered cytokine milieu that drive HSC myeloid skewing. Intestinal stem cell niches show decreased Wnt ligand secretion from Paneth cells, reducing LGR5+ ISC self-renewal capacity. Systemic pro-aging signals in the old circulation further impair niche function.

nodes.label: Cholesterol and Lipid Dysregulation
nodes.description: Age-related changes in lipid homeostasis include increased LDL and VLDL levels, decreased HDL function, increased small dense LDL susceptibility to oxidation, and impaired reverse cholesterol transport. Oxidized LDL (oxLDL) triggers endothelial inflammation, macrophage foam cell formation, and atherosclerotic plaque development. Intracellular cholesterol dysregulation contributes to neurodegeneration through impaired myelin maintenance and membrane raft signaling. APOE4 genotype impairs cholesterol clearance and is the strongest genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer's disease.

nodes.label: Impaired Mitochondrial Biogenesis
nodes.description: Mitochondrial biogenesis — the production of new mitochondria — declines with aging due to reduced PGC-1α/NRF1/TFAM axis activity, decreased NAD+ levels impairing SIRT1 activation of PGC-1α, and blunted AMPK signaling. This reduces mitochondrial mass and respiratory capacity in metabolically active tissues (skeletal muscle, heart, brain). Exercise is the most potent physiological inducer of mitochondrial biogenesis, explaining much of its anti-aging benefit. Impaired biogenesis combined with impaired mitophagy creates a net deficit in healthy mitochondrial mass.

nodes.label: cGAS-STING Pathway Activation
nodes.description: The cyclic GMP-AMP synthase (cGAS) - stimulator of interferon genes (STING) pathway is an innate immune sensor for cytosolic double-stranded DNA. In aging cells, cytosolic DNA derived from damaged nuclear DNA, dysfunctional mitochondria (mtDNA), reactivated retrotransposable elements (particularly LINE-1 elements), and micronuclei activates cGAS-STING, producing cGAMP that activates STING and downstream IRF3 and NF-κB signaling. This drives type I interferon and pro-inflammatory cytokine production, contributing to inflammaging and SASP. The cGAS-STING pathway links multiple primary hallmarks (genomic instability, mitochondrial dysfunction, epigenetic alterations) to inflammatory hallmarks.

nodes.label: Chaperone Network Decline
nodes.description: The cytosolic and ER chaperone networks (HSP70, HSP90, HSP40, GRP78/BiP, GRP94) facilitate protein folding, prevent aggregation, and target irreparably misfolded proteins for degradation. With aging, heat shock transcription factor 1 (HSF1)-mediated chaperone induction becomes blunted, reducing stress response capacity. Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) declines due to decreased LAMP2A receptor levels on lysosomes. Post-translational modifications (oxidation, glycation) compromise chaperone function. The combined decline in folding, refolding, and degradation capacity creates a proteostasis bottleneck that progressively worsens with age.

nodes.label: DNA Methylation Drift
nodes.description: Stochastic and systematic changes in DNA methylation patterns over time alter gene expression in aging tissues. Global hypomethylation, particularly at repetitive elements (LINE-1, Alu) and intergenic regions, can reactivate transposable elements and cause chromosomal instability. Locus-specific hypermethylation at tumor suppressor gene promoters drives cancer while hypermethylation at developmental gene promoters impairs tissue homeostasis. Methylation at CpG sites associated with immune gene regulation drives immunosenescence. The high fidelity required to maintain methylation across millions of CpG sites inevitably degrades through replication and repair, making methylation drift an unavoidable consequence of cell divisions.

nodes.label: Autophagic Flux Decline
nodes.description: Beyond specific autophagic pathways, the overall capacity for autophagic flux (cargo recognition → autophagosome formation → lysosome fusion → degradation → recycling) declines with age. Key rate-limiting steps include decreased ULK1/2 kinase activity (reduced AMPK signaling), impaired Beclin-1-dependent autophagosome nucleation, reduced ATG5-ATG12 conjugation efficiency, decreased LC3 lipidation, impaired RAB7-mediated autophagosome-lysosome fusion, and reduced lysosomal hydrolase activity. The net result is cargo backlog, reduced cellular recycling capacity, and accumulation of cellular debris that contributes to toxicity.

nodes.label: Skeletal Muscle Atrophy / Sarcopenia Pathways
nodes.description: Age-related loss of skeletal muscle mass and strength (sarcopenia) involves impaired protein synthesis (reduced mTORC1 anabolic signaling, blunted leucine/insulin responses), increased protein degradation (ubiquitin-proteasome system via MuRF1/MAFbx, autophagy), neuromuscular junction denervation, satellite cell dysfunction (reduced self-renewal and differentiation), increased intramuscular fat deposition, and mitochondrial dysfunction impairing energy supply for muscle contraction. Inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) directly inhibit muscle protein synthesis and promote atrophy through FOXO-driven atrogene expression. Sarcopenia accelerates frailty, fall risk, and metabolic dysfunction.

nodes.label: Dysregulated Bone Remodeling
nodes.description: Bone homeostasis requires balanced osteoblast (bone formation) and osteoclast (bone resorption) activity. With aging, this balance shifts toward net resorption: osteoblast function declines due to impaired Wnt signaling, increased adipogenic differentiation of mesenchymal stem cell progenitors, reduced growth hormone/IGF-1, and decreased sex hormones. Osteoclast activity is enhanced by inflammatory cytokines (RANKL/OPG imbalance, TNF-α, IL-6). Oxidative stress impairs osteoblast survival. Senescent osteocytes accumulate and disrupt local bone homeostasis through SASP. The net result is progressive bone loss (osteopenia→osteoporosis) with increased fracture risk.

nodes.label: Synaptic Dysfunction and Neuronal Loss
nodes.description: Age-related synaptic dysfunction manifests as reduced synaptic density, impaired long-term potentiation (LTP), decreased glutamate receptor (AMPA, NMDA) expression and function, altered calcium homeostasis, reduced synaptic vesicle availability, and impaired neurotransmitter release and recycling. Dendritic spine loss and retraction precede overt neuronal death. Synaptic dysfunction underlies cognitive decline and precedes neurodegeneration by decades. BDNF signaling, critical for synaptic plasticity, declines with age. Mitochondrial dysfunction in synaptic terminals impairs ATP supply for synaptic vesicle cycling and calcium extrusion.

nodes.label: Matrix Metalloproteinase / Proteolytic Imbalance
nodes.description: Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) and other extracellular proteases regulate ECM turnover, growth factor bioavailability, and cellular signaling. With aging and inflammation (particularly SASP-driven MMP secretion), MMP activity exceeds tissue inhibitor of metalloproteinase (TIMP) activity, driving pathological ECM remodeling. This manifests as cartilage degradation (osteoarthritis), plaque destabilization (atherosclerosis), emphysema (COPD), and tumor invasion. Concurrently, cross-linking and glycation of ECM proteins resists normal MMP-mediated remodeling, creating a paradox of concurrent stiffening and degradation depending on tissue context.

nodes.label: Hormonal Axis Dysregulation
nodes.description: Multiple endocrine axes decline with aging: growth hormone (GH) and IGF-1 (somatopause), sex hormones estrogen and testosterone (menopause/andropause), dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA), melatonin, and thyroid hormones. These hormones regulate anabolic processes, immune function, metabolism, and tissue maintenance. Their decline contributes to sarcopenia, osteoporosis, cognitive decline, cardiovascular risk, and impaired immune function. However, the relationship is complex — GH/IGF-1 reduction may be partly protective (reduced mTOR signaling). Sex hormone loss particularly drives rapid bone loss and cardiovascular risk increases in menopause.

nodes.label: Adipose Tissue Dysfunction
nodes.description: Aging adipose tissue undergoes profound remodeling: subcutaneous fat depots decrease while visceral fat expands; adipogenesis capacity of adipose progenitors declines; adipose tissue inflammation increases with macrophage infiltration and crown-like structure formation; adipokine secretion shifts from anti-inflammatory (adiponectin) to pro-inflammatory (leptin, resistin, visfatin). Ectopic fat deposition in liver, muscle, and other organs impairs insulin signaling and organ function. Senescent preadipocytes and adipocytes contribute to adipose SASP. These changes create a lipotoxic, pro-inflammatory systemic environment that drives metabolic and cardiovascular aging.

nodes.label: Glomerular Filtration Decline
nodes.description: Glomerular filtration rate (GFR) declines linearly after age 30-40, driven by glomerular hypertension (loss of autoregulation), glomerulosclerosis (progressive scarring of glomeruli), tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis, and reduced renal perfusion due to vascular aging. Oxidative stress, advanced glycation end-products, and inflammatory cytokines accelerate this decline. The resulting uremic milieu impairs clearance of small molecules, affects mineral metabolism (phosphate, FGF23, PTH axis), and creates a chronic inflammatory state. CKD itself accelerates cardiovascular aging through uremic toxin accumulation, endothelial dysfunction, and mineral-bone disorder.

nodes.label: Telomerase Insufficiency in Stem Cells
nodes.description: While most somatic cells lack significant telomerase activity, stem cells express telomerase to partially counteract telomere attrition. With aging, even stem cell telomerase activity declines, leading to gradual telomere shortening across stem cell compartments. When telomeres reach critically short lengths in stem cells, they undergo apoptosis or senescence rather than self-renewal. This progressive depletion of functional stem cells underlies organ regenerative failure. Rare human diseases with accelerated telomere shortening (dyskeratosis congenita, pulmonary fibrosis, aplastic anemia) demonstrate that telomerase insufficiency causes premature multi-organ failure resembling accelerated aging.

nodes.label: Axonal Transport Dysfunction
nodes.description: Neurons depend on axonal transport via kinesin (anterograde) and dynein-dynactin (retrograde) motors to shuttle organelles, vesicles, proteins, and RNA between cell bodies and synapses over distances of up to a meter. Aging impairs axonal transport through disrupted microtubule integrity (tau pathology, oxidative damage), reduced motor protein expression and processivity, mitochondrial dysfunction (reducing ATP availability for motor ATPases), and cargo-motor linkage defects. Impaired axonal transport leads to synaptic protein insufficiency, failure of damaged organelle return to soma for degradation, and retrograde axonopathy. Many neurodegenerative protein aggregates (TDP-43, FUS, SOD1) directly impair transport.

nodes.label: Alzheimer's Disease
nodes.description: Progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by amyloid-beta plaques, neurofibrillary tau tangles, synaptic loss, and widespread cortical neuronal death leading to dementia. The most common cause of dementia, with incidence doubling every five years after age 65.

nodes.label: Parkinson's Disease
nodes.description: Neurodegenerative disorder defined by loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and presence of Lewy body inclusions containing alpha-synuclein. Cardinal features include resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and postural instability.

nodes.label: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis
nodes.description: Fatal neurodegenerative disease of upper and lower motor neurons leading to progressive paralysis and respiratory failure. TDP-43 proteinopathy is present in >97% of cases; SOD1, FUS, and C9orf72 mutations account for most familial cases.

nodes.label: Vascular Dementia
nodes.description: Second most common cause of dementia, resulting from cumulative cerebrovascular injury including large vessel strokes, small vessel disease, white matter hyperintensities, and lacunar infarcts. Often co-occurs with Alzheimer's pathology.

nodes.label: Lewy Body Dementia
nodes.description: Second most common neurodegenerative dementia, characterized by fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, and REM sleep behavior disorder. Cortical and limbic Lewy bodies containing alpha-synuclein are the pathological substrate.

nodes.label: Huntington's Disease
nodes.description: Autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder caused by CAG repeat expansion in the HTT gene, producing mutant huntingtin protein that aggregates in striatal and cortical neurons. Manifests with chorea, cognitive decline, and psychiatric symptoms in midlife.

nodes.label: Multiple Sclerosis
nodes.description: Chronic autoimmune demyelinating disease of the CNS with progressive neurological disability. Autoreactive T and B cells attack myelin sheaths; with aging the progressive phase dominates due to neurodegeneration and failed remyelination rather than acute inflammation.

nodes.label: Late-Onset Epilepsy
nodes.description: New-onset seizures after age 60, frequently caused by cortical scarring from stroke, neurodegeneration, or tumors. Aging-associated changes in neuronal excitability, GABAergic inhibition decline, and increased cortical hyperexcitability contribute to seizure threshold lowering.

nodes.label: Atherosclerosis
nodes.description: Chronic inflammatory disease of large and medium arteries characterized by lipid-laden plaque formation in the intima. Foam cell accumulation, fibrous cap formation, and plaque rupture cause acute coronary syndromes and stroke. Accelerated by age-related endothelial dysfunction and systemic inflammation.

nodes.label: Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF)
nodes.description: Heart failure syndrome with normal or near-normal left ventricular ejection fraction but impaired diastolic filling due to myocardial stiffness. Driven by systemic inflammation, metabolic syndrome, and cardiac fibrosis. Predominantly affects older obese women and lacks approved disease-modifying therapies.

nodes.label: Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction (HFrEF)
nodes.description: Heart failure characterized by reduced left ventricular systolic function (EF <40%), typically following myocardial infarction or dilated cardiomyopathy. Cardiomyocyte loss, maladaptive remodeling, and neurohormonal activation drive progressive contractile failure.

nodes.label: Atrial Fibrillation
nodes.description: Most common cardiac arrhythmia, with prevalence rising steeply with age. Atrial fibrosis, electrical remodeling, and autonomic dysregulation create the substrate for chaotic atrial depolarization. Dramatically increases stroke risk and heart failure progression.

nodes.label: Hypertension
nodes.description: Sustained elevation of arterial blood pressure, the most prevalent age-related cardiovascular risk factor. Driven by arterial stiffening, impaired endothelial function, sympathetic activation, and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system dysregulation. Leads to target organ damage in heart, brain, and kidneys.

nodes.label: Coronary Artery Disease
nodes.description: Atherosclerotic narrowing of epicardial coronary arteries causing myocardial ischemia. Age is the dominant non-modifiable risk factor. Plaque rupture causes acute MI; stable angina from demand-supply mismatch in chronic disease.

nodes.label: Aortic Stenosis
nodes.description: Progressive calcification and fibrosis of the aortic valve leaflets leading to obstruction of left ventricular outflow. Most common valvular heart disease in the elderly; shares pathobiological features with atherosclerosis. Treated by surgical or transcatheter valve replacement.

nodes.label: Cardiac Hypertrophy
nodes.description: Pathological enlargement of the myocardium in response to chronic pressure or volume overload, neurohormonal activation, and direct aging effects. Maladaptive hypertrophy leads to fibrosis, diastolic dysfunction, and eventual heart failure.

nodes.label: Type 2 Diabetes
nodes.description: Metabolic disorder characterized by progressive insulin resistance, compensatory hyperinsulinemia, and eventual pancreatic beta-cell failure leading to hyperglycemia. Strongly age-associated; complications affect cardiovascular, renal, neurological, and ophthalmological systems.
pubmed_search_terms: T2D aging insulin resistance beta cell

nodes.label: Metabolic Syndrome
nodes.description: Cluster of interrelated metabolic abnormalities including central obesity, hypertriglyceridemia, low HDL, hypertension, and hyperglycemia. Substantially increases risk for T2D and cardiovascular disease. Prevalence rises sharply with age.

nodes.label: NAFLD/NASH
nodes.description: Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and its inflammatory form NASH represent a spectrum from hepatic steatosis to steatohepatitis, fibrosis, and cirrhosis. Increasingly common with aging, obesity, and metabolic syndrome; a leading cause of hepatocellular carcinoma.
pubmed_search_terms: nonalcoholic fatty liver aging inflammation aging

nodes.label: Obesity
nodes.description: Excess body fat accumulation, particularly visceral adiposity, that promotes systemic inflammation, insulin resistance, and accelerated biological aging. Age-related changes in appetite regulation, energy expenditure, and adipose biology promote weight gain.

nodes.label: Gout
nodes.description: Crystal arthropathy caused by monosodium urate deposition in joints, driven by hyperuricemia. Incidence rises steeply with age due to declining renal urate clearance, diuretic use, and metabolic syndrome. NLRP3 inflammasome activation by urate crystals drives acute attacks.

nodes.label: Hypothyroidism
nodes.description: Deficient thyroid hormone production causing metabolic slowing, fatigue, cognitive impairment, and cardiovascular risk. Prevalence increases with age; subclinical hypothyroidism is common. Autoimmune destruction (Hashimoto's) and iodine deficiency are leading causes.

nodes.label: Multiple Myeloma
nodes.description: Malignancy of plasma cells in the bone marrow producing monoclonal immunoglobulin. Median age at diagnosis is 69. Bone marrow microenvironment aging, genomic instability, and immunosenescence contribute to disease initiation and progression from MGUS.

nodes.label: Colorectal Cancer
nodes.description: Adenocarcinoma arising from colorectal epithelium, predominantly through chromosomal instability or microsatellite instability pathways. Incidence strongly age-related; cumulative DNA damage, dysbiosis, and impaired immune surveillance drive malignant transformation of adenomatous polyps.
pubmed_search_terms: colon cancer aging DNA damage dysbiosis

nodes.label: Lung Cancer
nodes.description: Leading cause of cancer mortality, predominantly non-small cell (NSCLC: adenocarcinoma, squamous cell) driven by tobacco carcinogen-induced DNA mutations. Age-related accumulation of somatic mutations and immunosenescence create permissive conditions for malignant transformation.

nodes.label: Breast Cancer
nodes.description: Most common cancer in women, with incidence rising with age. Hormonal changes at menopause, accumulated DNA damage in ductal epithelium, and altered stromal microenvironment all contribute. ER+ subtype predominates in postmenopausal women.

nodes.label: Prostate Cancer
nodes.description: Most common cancer in men, with incidence and lethal potential rising sharply with age. Age-related androgen signaling changes, accumulated somatic mutations, and senescent stromal cells drive malignant transformation of luminal epithelial cells.

nodes.label: Pancreatic Cancer
nodes.description: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is among the most lethal malignancies with five-year survival under 12%. Age-related KRAS mutations, chronic pancreatitis, and a highly immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment drive rapid progression.

nodes.label: Acute Myeloid Leukemia
nodes.description: Aggressive hematological malignancy arising from myeloid progenitor cells. Incidence increases markedly with age, frequently arising from clonal hematopoiesis. Associated with poor outcomes in the elderly due to comorbidities, frailty, and adverse cytogenetics.
pubmed_search_terms: acute myeloid leukemia aging clonal hematopoiesis

nodes.label: Melanoma
nodes.description: Malignancy of melanocytes driven by UV-induced DNA damage and somatic mutations (BRAF, NRAS). Incidence rises with cumulative UV exposure and aging. Immunosenescence impairs tumor immunosurveillance, allowing emergence of melanocytic malignancy.
pubmed_search_terms: melanoma aging UV DNA damage mechanisms

nodes.label: Bladder Cancer
nodes.description: Urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with strong age-dependence; median age at diagnosis is 73. Cumulative exposure to carcinogens (tobacco, aromatic amines) and chronic inflammation from recurrent infections drive mucosal DNA damage and malignant transformation.
pubmed_search_terms: bladder cancer aging mechanisms DNA damage

nodes.label: Sarcopenia
nodes.description: Progressive age-related loss of skeletal muscle mass, strength, and function. Affects >50% of adults over 80 and is a primary driver of frailty, disability, falls, and metabolic dysfunction. Interplay between anabolic resistance, neuromuscular denervation, and inflammation drives muscle wasting.
pubmed_search_terms: muscle wasting aging satellite cell mitochondria

nodes.label: Osteoporosis
nodes.description: Systemic skeletal disease characterized by reduced bone mineral density and microarchitectural deterioration, predisposing to fragility fractures. Major public health problem in aging populations; hip fractures carry high morbidity and mortality.
pubmed_search_terms: bone loss aging osteoblast osteoclast aging

nodes.label: Osteoarthritis
nodes.description: Degenerative joint disease characterized by articular cartilage loss, subchondral bone remodeling, osteophyte formation, and synovial inflammation. Most prevalent joint disorder in the elderly; strongly driven by cellular senescence in chondrocytes and synoviocytes.

nodes.label: Rheumatoid Arthritis
nodes.description: Chronic autoimmune inflammatory arthritis targeting synovial joints with systemic manifestations. Late-onset RA (>60 years) is common and often seronegative; immunosenescence, epigenetic alterations in T cells, and synoviocyte senescence contribute to joint destruction.

nodes.label: Frailty Syndrome
nodes.description: Geriatric syndrome of decreased physiological reserve and increased vulnerability to stressors, characterized by unintentional weight loss, exhaustion, weakness, slowness, and low activity. Represents a convergence of multiple aging hallmarks and is the most powerful predictor of adverse outcomes in older adults.
pubmed_search_terms: frailty syndrome aging SASP inflammation sarcopenia

nodes.label: Chronic Kidney Disease
nodes.description: Progressive and irreversible loss of kidney function with GFR <60 mL/min for >3 months. Age is a dominant risk factor; CKD accelerates systemic aging through uremic toxin accumulation, inflammation, and mineral-bone disorder.
pubmed_search_terms: chronic kidney disease aging mechanisms hallmarks

nodes.label: Diabetic Nephropathy
nodes.description: Leading cause of end-stage renal disease, caused by glomerular and tubular damage from hyperglycemia, AGE accumulation, and oxidative stress. Characterized by albuminuria, progressive GFR decline, and glomerular fibrosis.
pubmed_search_terms: diabetic nephropathy aging oxidative stress mechanisms

nodes.label: Renal Fibrosis
nodes.description: Final common pathway of virtually all progressive kidney diseases, characterized by tubular atrophy, glomerulosclerosis, and interstitial fibrosis driven by myofibroblast activation and ECM deposition. Irreversible loss of functional kidney architecture.

nodes.label: COPD
nodes.description: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease encompasses emphysema and chronic bronchitis, causing irreversible airflow limitation. Tobacco smoke-accelerated lung aging, with cellular senescence, elastin destruction, and impaired repair, are central mechanisms. Leading cause of respiratory morbidity.

nodes.label: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis
nodes.description: Progressive fibrotic lung disease of unknown cause predominantly affecting adults over 60. Accumulation of senescent alveolar type II epithelial cells, failure of regeneration, and aberrant fibroblast activation replace functional lung with scar tissue.
pubmed_search_terms: pulmonary fibrosis aging hallmarks cellular senescence

nodes.label: Pulmonary Hypertension
nodes.description: Elevated pulmonary arterial pressure from remodeling of small pulmonary arteries, leading to right heart failure. Age-related endothelial dysfunction, inflammation, and vascular smooth muscle cell senescence contribute to pulmonary vascular remodeling.

nodes.label: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
nodes.description: Repetitive upper airway obstruction during sleep causing intermittent hypoxia and sleep fragmentation. Prevalence increases with age and obesity. Intermittent hypoxia drives oxidative stress, systemic inflammation, and accelerates cardiovascular and metabolic aging.
pubmed_search_terms: sleep apnea aging oxidative stress inflammation
OSA aging mechanisms hallmarks intermittent hypoxia

nodes.label: Age-Related Macular Degeneration
nodes.description: Leading cause of blindness in the elderly, caused by degeneration of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and photoreceptors in the macula. Drusen accumulation, complement dysregulation, and neovascularization (wet AMD) drive progressive central vision loss.
pubmed_search_terms: age-related macular degeneration aging mechanisms hallmarks

nodes.label: Glaucoma
nodes.description: Progressive optic neuropathy with retinal ganglion cell loss, associated with elevated intraocular pressure. The second leading cause of blindness worldwide; age is the dominant risk factor. Mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress in RGCs and trabecular meshwork cells contribute to neurodegeneration.

nodes.label: Cataracts
nodes.description: Age-related opacification of the crystalline lens due to progressive misfolding and aggregation of crystallin proteins. Lens epithelial cells are among the longest-lived non-dividing cells; their lifetime accumulation of oxidative damage and protein aggregation makes cataract a canonical proteostasis disease.

nodes.label: Diabetic Retinopathy
nodes.description: Microvascular complication of diabetes causing pericyte loss, retinal ischemia, neovascularization, and vision loss. Hyperglycemia-driven oxidative stress, inflammation, and AGE accumulation damage retinal vasculature. Leading cause of blindness in working-age adults.
pubmed_search_terms: diabetic retinopathy aging mechanisms oxidative stress

nodes.label: Skin Aging and Photoaging
nodes.description: Cumulative intrinsic and UV-driven skin aging manifesting as wrinkles, loss of elasticity, hyperpigmentation, and impaired wound healing. Loss of dermal fibroblast function, collagen degradation, and accumulation of senescent cells are primary mechanisms.

nodes.label: Psoriasis (Late-Onset)
nodes.description: Chronic immune-mediated inflammatory skin disease with keratinocyte hyperproliferation and T cell-driven skin inflammation. Late-onset psoriasis (>40 years) shows distinct immunological features related to immunosenescence and inflammaging.

nodes.label: Impaired Wound Healing
nodes.description: Age-related decline in wound healing capacity manifesting as chronic non-healing ulcers. Reduced keratinocyte and fibroblast proliferative capacity, impaired immune cell recruitment, senescent cells impairing regeneration, and poor vascularization all contribute.
pubmed_search_terms: chronic wound aging senescence stem cell

nodes.label: Immunosenescence
nodes.description: Age-related decline and dysregulation of the immune system characterized by thymic involution, T cell repertoire contraction, impaired vaccine responses, and paradoxical chronic inflammation. Drives susceptibility to infections, cancer, and autoimmunity.
pubmed_search_terms: thymic involution aging T cell aging

nodes.label: Autoimmune Thyroiditis (Hashimoto's)
nodes.description: Chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis driven by autoreactive T and B cells destroying thyroid follicular cells. Prevalence increases with age; immunosenescence-associated breakdown of self-tolerance underlies late-onset presentation.

nodes.label: Late-Onset Autoimmune Diabetes (LADA)
nodes.description: Slowly progressive autoimmune diabetes in adults, sharing features of type 1 and type 2 diabetes. Immunosenescence may paradoxically permit slow immune-mediated beta-cell destruction while metabolic stress and aging accelerate progression.
pubmed_search_terms: LADA late autoimmune diabetes aging mechanisms

nodes.label: Late-Onset Depression
nodes.description: Major depressive disorder with onset after age 60, associated with vascular changes, neuroinflammation, hippocampal volume loss, and HPA axis dysregulation. Distinct from early-onset depression in its biological substrate and poor treatment response.

nodes.label: Late-Onset Anxiety
nodes.description: Generalized anxiety disorder and anxiety syndromes arising or persisting in older age. Driven by neurobiological changes including amygdala hyperreactivity, prefrontal decline, HPA axis hyperactivation, and neuroinflammation interacting with psychosocial stressors.

nodes.label: Dementia-Related Psychosis
nodes.description: Psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations) occurring in the context of neurodegenerative dementia, particularly Lewy body dementia, Parkinson's dementia, and Alzheimer's disease. Dopaminergic and cholinergic imbalance, neuroinflammation, and cortical Lewy body burden are key contributors.

nodes.label: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
nodes.description: Chronic relapsing inflammatory disorders of the gastrointestinal tract including Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. Late-onset IBD (>60 years) is increasingly recognized; aging-associated dysbiosis, intestinal barrier impairment, and immunosenescence contribute to a distinct pathobiology.
pubmed_search_terms: inflammatory bowel disease aging mechanisms dysbiosis

nodes.label: Colorectal Polyps
nodes.description: Premalignant adenomatous polyps of the colorectum arising from accumulated somatic mutations and epigenetic alterations in colonocytes. Incidence rises sharply with age; serrated and advanced adenomas carry highest cancer risk.

nodes.label: Hepatic Fibrosis
nodes.description: Excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix proteins in the liver resulting from chronic injury or NASH, leading to cirrhosis and liver failure. Hepatic stellate cell activation by TGF-β, oxidative stress, and SASP factors drives progressive fibrosis.

nodes.label: Barrett's Esophagus
nodes.description: Metaplastic replacement of esophageal squamous epithelium by intestinal-type columnar epithelium in response to chronic acid reflux. Age-related reduction in lower esophageal sphincter tone and increased prevalence of GERD drive Barrett's metaplasia and adenocarcinoma risk.
pubmed_search_terms: esophageal metaplasia aging senescence oxidative stress

nodes.label: Menopause-Related Conditions
nodes.description: Cluster of conditions arising from ovarian estrogen depletion at menopause including vasomotor symptoms, urogenital atrophy, accelerated bone loss, and cardiovascular risk increase. Estrogen loss accelerates multiple aging processes simultaneously.

nodes.label: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia
nodes.description: Non-malignant enlargement of the prostate gland causing lower urinary tract symptoms in aging men. Driven by age-related hormonal changes (DHT/estrogen ratio), prostatic stromal senescence, and inflammation creating a proliferative microenvironment.
pubmed_search_terms: benign prostatic hyperplasia aging inflammation hallmarks

nodes.label: Erectile Dysfunction
nodes.description: Inability to achieve or maintain a penile erection sufficient for sexual intercourse. Prevalence increases dramatically with age, driven by endothelial dysfunction, reduced NO bioavailability, hypogonadism, and autonomic neuropathy.

nodes.label: Clonal Hematopoiesis of Indeterminate Potential (CHIP)
nodes.description: Age-related expansion of hematopoietic stem cell clones carrying somatic mutations (DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1) that confer clonal fitness advantages. Present in >10% of adults >70; associated with hematological malignancies, cardiovascular disease, and all-cause mortality.
pubmed_search_terms: CHIP aging clonal hematopoiesis mechanisms hallmarks

nodes.label: Anemia of Aging
nodes.description: Unexplained normocytic anemia in older adults not attributable to nutritional deficiency, renal disease, or bone marrow failure. Driven by impaired erythropoiesis, chronic low-grade inflammation, and declining hematopoietic stem cell function with age.
pubmed_search_terms: anemia of aging mechanisms hematopoiesis inflammation
age-related anemia stem cell exhaustion aging

nodes.label: Thrombocytopenia of Aging
nodes.description: Age-associated reduction in platelet counts due to declining megakaryopoiesis, increased platelet clearance, and immune dysregulation. Subclinical thrombocytopenia increases bleeding risk and may reflect impaired HSC and megakaryocyte progenitor function.
pubmed_search_terms: platelet decline aging bone marrow mechanisms

nodes.label: Adrenal Insufficiency
nodes.description: Deficient glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid production from adrenal cortex atrophy or autoimmune destruction. Aging-related adrenal atrophy reduces reserve capacity; DHEA and cortisol dynamics are altered with age. Autoimmune Addison's disease prevalence increases with immunosenescence.

nodes.label: Growth Hormone Deficiency (Somatopause)
nodes.description: Age-related decline in growth hormone secretion and IGF-1 levels (somatopause) contributing to sarcopenia, adiposity, impaired immune function, and frailty. GH pulse amplitude and frequency decline progressively after age 30.
pubmed_search_terms: somatopause aging GH IGF-1 decline mechanisms
growth hormone deficiency aging sarcopenia frailty

nodes.label: Ischemic Stroke
nodes.description: Acute focal brain infarction due to thromboembolism or small vessel occlusion. Incidence doubles each decade after 55. Atherosclerosis, atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and clonal hematopoiesis are the dominant age-amplified risk factors. Post-stroke neurodegeneration accelerates cognitive decline.

nodes.label: Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy
nodes.description: Most common chronic complication of diabetes, affecting >50% of diabetic patients over time. Hyperglycemia-driven oxidative stress, advanced glycation end-products, and impaired axonal transport in peripheral neurons lead to length-dependent sensorimotor neuropathy.
pubmed_search_terms: diabetic neuropathy aging mechanisms oxidative stress
